---
title: ""
---

```{=html}
<style>
/* ===== Home page styling ===== */
.hero {
  text-align: center;
  padding: 4.5rem 1rem 3rem 1rem;
  margin-bottom: 1.5rem;
}

.hero h1 {
  font-size: 2.8rem;
  font-weight: 700;
  margin-bottom: 0.8rem;
  line-height: 1.15;
}

.hero .subtitle {
  font-size: 1.15rem;
  color: #5f6b7a;
  max-width: 850px;
  margin: 0 auto;
  line-height: 1.8;
}

.section-block {
  max-width: 1050px;
  margin: 0 auto 2.2rem auto;
  padding: 2rem 2rem;
  background: #ffffff;
  border: 1px solid #e9edf2;
  border-radius: 18px;
  box-shadow: 0 6px 20px rgba(0,0,0,0.04);
}

.section-block h2 {
  font-size: 1.45rem;
  margin-bottom: 1rem;
  border-bottom: 1px solid #edf1f5;
  padding-bottom: 0.6rem;
}

.section-block p {
  line-height: 1.9;
  color: #2f3a45;
  margin-bottom: 0;
}

.interests {
  display: flex;
  flex-wrap: wrap;
  gap: 12px;
  margin-top: 1rem;
}

.interest-tag {
  display: inline-block;
  padding: 0.65rem 1rem;
  border-radius: 999px;
  background: #f4f7fb;
  border: 1px solid #dde6f0;
  font-size: 0.98rem;
  color: #334155;
}

.news-box {
  padding: 1rem 1.2rem;
  background: #f8fafc;
  border-left: 4px solid #94a3b8;
  border-radius: 10px;
  line-height: 1.8;
  color: #334155;
}

.home-note {
  text-align: center;
  color: #7a8594;
  font-size: 0.95rem;
  margin-top: 2.5rem;
  margin-bottom: 1rem;
}
</style>
```

::: hero
<h1>Welcome to Yong-Yeon Cho’s Lab</h1>

<p class="subtitle">

We study the molecular mechanisms that regulate cellular architecture, membrane-associated signaling, and stress-responsive transcriptional programs.

</p>
:::

::: section-block
## About the Lab

Our laboratory has long been engaged in research on cancer signal transduction, protein stability regulation, and regulated cell death. In recent years, our work has primarily focused on molecular stability control associated with tumorigenesis and therapeutic resistance. We have systematically elucidated the roles of signaling networks involving RSK2, FBXW7, STAT2, ERK3, and ELK3 in cancer cell proliferation and transformation.

Concurrently, we have established and advanced a novel research framework for **karyoptosis**, a form of regulated cell death, centered on nuclear envelope homeostasis and aberrant cleavage of CREB3. Furthermore, we continue to expand our investigations into innate immune and inflammatory pathways, including NLRP3, cGAS/STING, and necroptosis, as well as natural product pharmacology, drug metabolism, and translational toxicology. Together, these efforts define a distinctive research profile that integrates fundamental molecular mechanisms with pharmaceutical applications.
:::

:::: section-block
## Research Interests

::: interests
[Nuclear membrane biology]{.interest-tag} [Stress-responsive transcription factors]{.interest-tag} [Regulated intramembrane proteolysis]{.interest-tag} [Structural and computational biology]{.interest-tag} [Karyoptosis]{.interest-tag} [Protein stability]{.interest-tag}
:::
::::

:::: section-block
## News

::: news-box
Our lab website is currently under construction.\
Updates on new projects, publications, and lab activities will be posted here soon.
:::
::::

::: home-note
Exploring molecular mechanisms from fundamental biology to translational application.
:::
